1→## Why should I use ktlint?
  2→
  3→the short answer is **Simplicity**.
  4→
  5→Spending time on configuration (and maintenance down the road) of hundred-line long style config file(s) is counter-productive. Instead of wasting your energy on something that has no business value - focus on what really matters (not debating whether to use tabs or spaces).
  6→
  7→By using ktlint you put the importance of code clarity and community conventions over personal preferences. This makes things easier for people reading your code as well as frees you from having to document and explain what style potential contributor(s) have to follow.
  8→
  9→ktlint is a single binary with both linter & formatter included. All you need is to drop it in (no need to get [overwhelmed](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Decision_fatigue) while choosing among [dozens of code style options](https://checkstyle.sourceforge.net/checks.html)).
 10→
 11→##  How do I enable or disable a rule?
 12→
 13→An individual rule can be enabled or disabled with a rule property. The name of the rule property consists of the `ktlint_` prefix followed by the rule set id followed by a `_` and the rule id. Examples:
 14→```editorconfig
 15→ktlint_standard_final-newline = disabled # Disables the `final-newline` rule in the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
 16→ktlint_standard_some-experimental-rule = enabled # Enables the (experimental) `some-experimental-rule` in the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
 17→ktlint_custom-rule-set_custom-rule = disabled # Disables the `custom-rule` rule in the `custom-rule-set` rule set (not provided by KtLint)
 18→```
 19→
 20→!!! note
 21→    The *rule* properties are applied after applying the *rule set* properties and take precedence. So if a rule set is disabled but a specific rule of that rule set is enabled, then the rule will be executed.
 22→
 23→##  How do I enable or disable a rule set?
 24→
 25→All rules in a rule set can be enabled or disabled with a rule set property. The name of the rule set property consists of the `ktlint_` prefix followed by the rule set id. Examples:
 26→```editorconfig
 27→ktlint_standard = disabled # Disable all rules from the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
 28→ktlint_experimental = enabled # Enable rules marked as experimental for all rule sets that are enabled
 29→ktlint_custom-rule-set = enabled # Enable all rules in the `custom-rule-set` rule set (not provided by KtLint)
 30→```
 31→
 32→!!! note
 33→    All rules from the `standard` and custom rule sets are *enabled* by default and can optionally be disabled in the `.editorconfig`. All `experimental` rules are *disabled* by default and can optionally be enabled in the `.editorconfig`.
 34→
 35→## Can I have my own rules on top of ktlint?
 36→
 37→Absolutely, "no configuration" doesn't mean "no extensibility". You can add your own ruleset(s) to discover potential bugs, check for anti-patterns, etc.
 38→
 39→See [adding a custom rule set](../api/custom-rule-set/) for more information.
 40→
 41→## How do I suppress errors for a line/block/file?
 42→
 43→!!! tip
 44→    Suppressing a `ktlint` violation is meant primarily as an escape latch for the rare cases when **ktlint** is not able to produce the correct result. Please report any such instances using [GitHub Issues](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues)).
 45→
 46→To disable a specific rule you'll need the rule identifier which is displayed at the end of the lint error.
 47→
 48→An error can be suppressed using:
 49→
 50→* EOL comments
 51→* Block comments
 52→* @Suppress annotations
 53→
 54→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Suppress annotation"
 55→
 56→    ```kotlin
 57→    // Suppressing all rules for the entire file
 58→    @file:Suppress("ktlint")
 59→
 60→    // Suppress a single rule (with id 'rule-id', defined in rule set with id 'rule-set-id') from the annotated construct
 61→    @Suppress("ktlint:rule-set-id:rule-id")
 62→    class Foo {}
 63→
 64→    // Suppress multiple rules for the annotated construct
 65→    @Suppress("ktlint:standard:no-wildcard-imports", "ktlint:custom-rule-set-id:custom-rule-id")
 66→    import foo.*
 67→
 68→    // Suppress all rules for the annotated construct
 69→    @Suppress("ktlint")
 70→    import foo.*
 71→    ```
 72→=== "[:material-heart:](#) EOL comments"
 73→
 74→    ```kotlin
 75→    // Suppress a single rule for the commented line
 76→    import foo.* // ktlint-disable standard_no-wildcard-imports
 77→
 78→    // Suppress multiple rules for the commented line
 79→    import foo.* // ktlint-disable standard_no-wildcard-imports standard_other-rule-id
 80→
 81→    // Suppress all rules for the commented line
 82→    import foo.* // ktlint-disable
 83→    ```
 84→
 85→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Block comments"
 86→
 87→    ```kotlin
 88→    // Suppress a single rule for all code between the start and end tag
 89→    /* ktlint-disable standard_no-wildcard-imports */
 90→    import foo.*
 91→    /* ktlint-disable standard_no-wildcard-imports */
 92→
 93→    // Suppress multiple rules for all code between the start and end tag
 94→    /* ktlint-disable standard_no-wildcard-imports standard_no-wildcard-imports */
 95→    import foo.*
 96→    /* ktlint-enable standard_no-wildcard-imports standard_no-wildcard-imports */
 97→
 98→    // Suppress all rules for all code between the start and end tag
 99→    /* ktlint-disable */
100→    import foo.*
101→    /* ktlint-enable */
102→    ```
103→
104→!!! important
105→    When using the block comments, the `ktlint-enable` directive needs to specify the exact same rule-id's and in the same order as the `ktlint-disable` directive.
106→
107→!!! warning
108→    From a consistency perspective seen, it might be best to **not** mix the (EOL/Block) comment style with the annotation style in the same project.
109→
110→## How do I globally disable a rule without `.editorconfig`?
111→
112→When using Ktlint CLI, you may pass a list of disabled rules via the `--disabled_rules` command line flag. The value is a comma separated list of rule id's that have to be disabled. The rule id must be fully qualified (e.g. must be prefixed with the rule set id). 
113→
114→
115→## Why is `.editorconfig` property `disabled_rules` deprecated and how do I resolve this?
116→
117→The `.editorconfig` properties `disabled_rules` and `ktlint_disabled_rules` are deprecated as of KtLint version `0.48` and are removed in version `0.49`. Those properties contain a comma separated list of rules which are disabled. Using a comma separated list of values has some disadvantages.
118→
119→A big disadvantage is that it is not possible to override the property partially in an `.editorconfig` file in a subpackage. Another disadvantage is that it is not possible to express explicitly that a rule is enabled. Lastly, (qualified) rule ids can be 20 characters or longer, which makes a list with multiple entries hard to read.
120→
121→Starting with KtLint `0.48` entire rule sets and individual rules can be disabled / enabled with a separate property per rule (set). Examples:
122→```editorconfig
123→ktlint_standard = disabled # Disable all rules from the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
124→ktlint_standard_final-newline = enabled # Enables the `final-newline` rule in the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
125→ktlint_experimental = enabled # Enable rules marked as experimental for all rule sets that are enabled
126→ktlint_standard_some-experimental-rule = disabled # Disables the (experimental) `some-experimental-rule` in the `standard` rule set provided by KtLint
127→ktlint_custom-rule-set = enabled # Enable all rules in the `custom-rule-set` rule set (not provided by KtLint)
128→ktlint_custom-rule-set_custom-rule = disabled # Disables the `custom-rule` rule in the `custom-rule-set` rule set (not provided by KtLint)
129→```
130→
131→!!! note
132→    All rules from the `standard` and custom rule sets are *enabled* by default and can optionally be disabled in the `.editorconfig`. All `experimental` rules are *disabled* by default and can optionally be enabled in the `.editorconfig`.
133→
134→!!! note
135→    The *rule* properties are applied after applying the *rule set* properties and take precedence. So if a rule set is disabled but a specific rule of that rule set is enabled, then the rule will be executed.  
136→
137→## Why is wildcard import `java.util.*` not reported by the `no-wildcard-imports` rule?
138→
139→The `no-wildcard-imports` rule forbids wildcard imports, except for imports defined in `.editorconfig` property `ij_kotlin_packages_to_use_import_on_demand`. If this property is not explicitly set, it allows wildcards imports like `java.util.*` by default to keep in sync with IntelliJ IDEA behavior.
140→
141→## Can a new toggle be added to optionally (enable/disable) format code in a particular way?
142→
143→Ktlint can be configured by enabling and disabling rules. Some rules can be configured in more details with additional `.editorconfig` properties. Regularly, a new configuration option is requested to modify behavior in existing rules.
144→
145→Ktlint is restrictive with adding additional configuration settings to customize behavior in rules. Each configuration option that Ktlint offers comes with complexity that has to be maintained by only a couple of maintainers. As of that, we cannot provide tens or even hundreds of such options.
146→
147→Less configuration options also means less discussions in teams about settings to use. Unfortunately this means that you cannot tweak Ktlint exactly to the format you prefer.
148→
149→!!! tip
150→    Any idea for a new configuration option is valuable. Please create an issue for it so that it can be considered to incorporate it in Ktlint.
151→
152→## Can I use KtLint to directly format the code I'm generating with KotlinPoet?
153→
154→Yes, it is possible to use KtLint to directly format the code generated with KotlinPoet. 
155→To do so, you must include the dependencies `com.pinterest.ktlint:ktlint-core` and `com.pinterest.ktlint:ktlint-ruleset-standard` in your Gradle/Maven project.
156→
157→!!! warning
158→    Do not include the dependency `com.pinterest:ktlint` as that would import the entire ktlint project including unwanted dependencies. Besides a much bigger artifact, it might also result in problems regarding logging.
159→
160→To format the output of KotlinPoet with KtLint, you can use the following snippet:
161→
162→```kotlin
163→val ruleProviders = buildSet {
164→  ServiceLoader
165→      .load(RuleSetProviderV2::class.java)
166→      .flatMapTo(this) { it.getRuleProviders() }
167→}
168→val ktLintRuleEngine = KtLintRuleEngine(
169→  ruleProviders = ruleProviders,
170→  editorConfigDefaults = EditorConfigDefaults.load(EDITORCONFIG_PATH),
171→)
172→ktLintRuleEngine.format(outputDir.toPath())
173→```
174→Here, outputDir refers to the directory of the generated files by KotlinPoet, ktLintRuleEngine is an instance of KtLint rule engine.
175→
176→It is also possible to format file-by-file the output of KotlinPoet if you write your `FileSpec` to a `StringBuilder()`, instead of a `File`, 
177→and send the generated code as `String` to KtLint inside a `CodeSnippet`:
178→```kotlin
179→kotlinFile.writeText(
180→  ktLintRuleEngine.format(
181→    Code.CodeSnippet(
182→      stringBuilder.toString()
183→    )
184→  )
185→)
186→```
187→
188→# Are formatter tags respected?
189→
190→As of version `0.49.x` the formatter tags of IntelliJ IDEA are respected. By default, those formatter tags are disabled. The formatter tags can be enabled with `.editorconfig` properties below:
191→```editorconfig
192→ij_formatter_tags_enabled = true # Defaults to 'false'
193→ij_formatter_off_tag = some-custom-off-tag # Defaults to '@formatter:off'
194→ij_formatter_on_tag = some-custom-on-tag # Defaults to '@formatter:on'
195→```
196→
197→When enabled, the ktlint rule checking is disabled for all code surrounded by the formatter tags.
198→

1→## Annotation formatting
   2→
   3→Multiple annotations should be on a separate line than the annotated declaration; annotations with parameters should each be on separate lines; annotations should be followed by a space
   4→
   5→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
   6→
   7→    ```kotlin
   8→    // A single annotation (without parameters) is allowed on same line as annotated construct
   9→    @FunctionalInterface class FooBar {
  10→        @JvmField var foo: String
  11→        @Test fun bar() {}
  12→    }
  13→    // A class or function parameter may have a single annotation with parameter(s) on the same line
  14→    class Foo(@Path("fooId") val fooId: String)
  15→    class Bar(
  16→        @NotNull("fooId") val fooId: String,
  17→        @NotNull("bar") bar: String
  18→    )
  19→    // Multiple annotations (without parameters) are allowed on the same line
  20→    @Foo @Bar
  21→    class FooBar {
  22→        @Foo @Bar
  23→        var foo: String
  24→        @Foo @Bar
  25→        fun bar() {}
  26→    }
  27→    // An array of annotations (without parameters) is allowed on same line as annotated construct
  28→    @[Foo Bar] class FooBar2 {
  29→        @[Foo Bar] var foo: String
  30→        @[Foo Bar] fun bar() {}
  31→    }
  32→    ```
  33→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
  34→
  35→    ```kotlin
  36→    // An annotation with parameter(s) is not allowed on same line as annotated construct
  37→    @Suppress("Unused") class FooBar {
  38→        @Suppress("Unused") var foo: String
  39→        @Suppress("Unused") fun bar() {}
  40→    }
  41→    // Multiple annotation on same line as annotated construct are not allowed
  42→    @Foo @Bar class FooBar {
  43→        @Foo @Bar var foo: String
  44→        @Foo @Bar fun bar() {}
  45→    }
  46→    ```
  47→
  48→Rule-id: `annotation` (`standard` rule set)
  49→
  50→## Argument list wrapping
  51→
  52→All arguments should be on the same line, or every argument should be on a separate line.
  53→
  54→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
  55→
  56→    ```kotlin
  57→    val x = f(
  58→        a,
  59→        b,
  60→        c
  61→    )
  62→    ```
  63→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
  64→
  65→    ```kotlin
  66→    val x = f(
  67→        a,
  68→        b, c
  69→    )
  70→    ```
  71→
  72→Rule-id: `argument-list-wrapping` (`standard` rule set)
  73→
  74→## Block comment initial star alignment
  75→
  76→Lines in a block comment which (exclusive the indentation) start with a `*` should have this `*` aligned with the `*` in the opening of the block comment.
  77→
  78→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
  79→
  80→    ```kotlin
  81→    /*
  82→     * This comment is formatted well.
  83→     */
  84→    ```
  85→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
  86→
  87→    ```kotlin
  88→    /*
  89→          * This comment is not formatted well.
  90→        */
  91→    ```
  92→
  93→Rule id: `block-comment-initial-star-alignment` (`standard` rule set)
  94→
  95→## Chain wrapping
  96→
  97→When wrapping chained calls `.`, `?.` and `?:` should be placed on the next line
  98→
  99→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 100→
 101→    ```kotlin
 102→    val foo = listOf(1, 2, 3)
 103→        .filter { it > 2 }!!
 104→        .takeIf { it.count() > 100 }
 105→        ?.sum()
 106→    val foobar = foo()
 107→        ?: bar
 108→    ```
 109→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 110→
 111→    ```kotlin
 112→    val foo = listOf(1, 2, 3).
 113→        filter { it > 2 }!!.
 114→        takeIf { it.count() > 100 }?.
 115→        sum()
 116→    val foobar = foo() ?:
 117→        bar
 118→    ```
 119→
 120→Rule id: `chain-wrapping` (`standard` rule set)
 121→
 122→## Class/object naming
 123→
 124→Enforce naming of class.
 125→
 126→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 127→
 128→    ```kotlin
 129→    class Foo
 130→    class Foo1
 131→    ```
 132→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint JUnit Test"
 133→
 134→    ```kotlin
 135→    @Nested
 136→    inner class `Some descriptive class name` {
 137→        @Test
 138→        fun `Some descriptive test name`() {
 139→            // do something
 140→        }
 141→    }
 142→    ```
 143→
 144→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 145→
 146→    ```kotlin
 147→    class foo
 148→    class Foo_Bar
 149→    class `Some class in the production code`
 150→    ```
 151→
 152→!!! note
 153→    Functions in files which import a class from package `org.junit.jupiter.api` are considered to be test functions and are allowed to have a name specified between backticks and do not need to adhere to the normal naming convention. Although, the [Kotlin coding conventions](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/coding-conventions.html) does not allow this explicitly for class identifiers, `ktlint` does allow it.
 154→
 155→This rule can also be suppressed with the IntelliJ IDEA inspection suppression `ClassName`.
 156→
 157→Rule id: `class-naming` (`standard` rule set)
 158→
 159→## Enum entry
 160→
 161→Enum entry names should be uppercase underscore-separated or upper camel-case separated.
 162→
 163→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 164→
 165→    ```kotlin
 166→    enum class Bar {
 167→        FOO,
 168→        Foo,
 169→        FOO_BAR,
 170→        FooBar
 171→    }
 172→    ```
 173→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 174→
 175→    ```kotlin
 176→    enum class Bar {
 177→        foo,
 178→        bAr,
 179→        Foo_Bar,
 180→    }
 181→    ```
 182→
 183→Rule id: `enum-entry-name-case` (`standard` rule set)
 184→
 185→## File name
 186→
 187→A file containing only one visible (e.g. non-private) class, and visible declarations related to that class only, should be named according to that element. The same applies if the file does not contain a visible class but exactly one type alias or one object declaration. Otherwise, the PascalCase notation should be used.
 188→
 189→Rule id: `filename` (`standard` rule set)
 190→
 191→## Final newline
 192→
 193→Ensures consistent usage of a newline at the end of each file. 
 194→
 195→This rule can be configured with `.editorconfig` property [`insert_final_newline`](../configuration-ktlint/#final-newline).
 196→
 197→Rule id: `final-newline` (`standard` rule set)
 198→
 199→## Import ordering
 200→
 201→Ensures that imports are ordered consistently (see [Import Layouts](../configuration-ktlint/#import-layouts) for configuration).
 202→
 203→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 204→
 205→    ```kotlin
 206→    import com.bar.Bar
 207→    import com.foo.Foo
 208→    import org.foo.bar.FooBar
 209→    import java.util.concurrent.ConcurrentHashMap
 210→    ```
 211→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 212→
 213→    ```kotlin
 214→    import com.bar.Bar
 215→    import java.util.concurrent.ConcurrentHashMap
 216→    import org.foo.bar.FooBar
 217→    import com.foo.Foo
 218→    ```
 219→
 220→Rule id: `import-ordering` (`standard` rule set)
 221→
 222→## Indentation
 223→
 224→Indentation formatting - respects `.editorconfig` `indent_size` with no continuation indent (see [EditorConfig](../configuration-ktlint/) section for more).
 225→
 226→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 227→
 228→    ```kotlin
 229→    fun main() {
 230→        foobar(
 231→            a,
 232→            b,
 233→            c
 234→        )
 235→    }
 236→    ```
 237→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 238→
 239→    ```kotlin
 240→    fun main() {
 241→        foobar(
 242→              a,
 243→              b,
 244→              c
 245→              )
 246→    }
 247→    ```
 248→
 249→!!! note
 250→    This rule handles indentation for many different language constructs which can not be summarized with a few examples. See the [unit tests](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/blob/master/ktlint-ruleset-standard/src/test/kotlin/com/pinterest/ktlint/ruleset/standard/rules/IndentationRuleTest.kt) for more details.
 251→
 252→Rule id: `indent` (`standard` rule set)
 253→
 254→## Max line length
 255→
 256→Ensures that lines do not exceed the given length of `.editorconfig` property `max_line_length` (see [EditorConfig](../configuration-ktlint/) section for more). This rule does not apply in a number of situations. For example, in the case a line exceeds the maximum line length due to a comment that disables ktlint rules then that comment is being ignored when validating the length of the line. The `.editorconfig` property `ktlint_ignore_back_ticked_identifier` can be set to ignore identifiers which are enclosed in backticks, which for example is very useful when you want to allow longer names for unit tests.
 257→
 258→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 259→
 260→    ```kotlin
 261→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
 262→    // at the X character on the right           X
 263→    // Lines below are accepted although the max
 264→    // line length is exceeded.
 265→    package com.toooooooooooooooooooooooooooo.long
 266→    import com.tooooooooooooooooooooooooooooo.long
 267→    val foo =
 268→        """
 269→        fooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooo
 270→        """
 271→    @Test
 272→    fun `Test description which is toooooooooooo long`() {
 273→    }
 274→    ```
 275→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 276→
 277→    ```kotlin
 278→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
 279→    // at the X character on the right           X
 280→    val fooooooooooooooo = "fooooooooooooooooooooo"
 281→    val foooooooooooooo = "foooooooooooooooooooo" // some comment
 282→    val fooooooooooooo =
 283→        "foooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooooo"
 284→    ```
 285→
 286→Rule id: `max-line-length` (`standard` rule set)
 287→
 288→## Modifier order
 289→
 290→Consistent order of modifiers
 291→
 292→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 293→
 294→    ```kotlin
 295→    abstract class A {
 296→        protected open val v = ""
 297→        internal open suspend fun f(v: Any): Any = ""
 298→        protected lateinit var lv: String
 299→    }
 300→    ```
 301→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 302→
 303→    ```kotlin
 304→    abstract class A {
 305→        open protected val v = ""
 306→        open suspend internal fun f(v: Any): Any = ""
 307→        lateinit protected var lv: String
 308→    }
 309→    ```
 310→
 311→Rule id: `modifier-order` (`standard` rule set)
 312→
 313→## Multiline if-else
 314→
 315→Braces required for multiline if/else statements.
 316→
 317→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 318→
 319→    ```kotlin
 320→    val foo =
 321→        if (true) {
 322→            return 0
 323→        } else {
 324→            return 1
 325→        }
 326→    ```
 327→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 328→
 329→    ```kotlin
 330→    val foo =
 331→        if (true)
 332→            return 0
 333→        else
 334→            return 1
 335→    ```
 336→
 337→Rule id: `multiline-if-else` (`standard` rule set)
 338→
 339→## No blank lines before `}`
 340→
 341→No blank lines before `}`.
 342→
 343→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 344→
 345→    ```kotlin
 346→    fun main() {
 347→        fun a() {
 348→        }
 349→        fun b()
 350→    }
 351→    ```
 352→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 353→
 354→    ```kotlin
 355→    fun main() {
 356→        fun a() {
 357→
 358→        }
 359→        fun b()
 360→
 361→    }
 362→    ```
 363→
 364→Rule id: `no-blank-line-before-rbrace` (`standard` rule set)
 365→
 366→## No blank lines in chained method calls
 367→
 368→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 369→
 370→    ```kotlin
 371→    fun foo(inputText: String) {
 372→        inputText
 373→            .lowercase(Locale.getDefault())
 374→    }
 375→    ```
 376→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 377→
 378→    ```kotlin
 379→    fun foo(inputText: String) {
 380→        inputText
 381→
 382→            .lowercase(Locale.getDefault())
 383→    }
 384→    ```
 385→
 386→Rule id: `no-blank-lines-in-chained-method-calls` (`standard` rule set)
 387→
 388→## No consecutive blank lines
 389→
 390→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 391→
 392→    ```kotlin
 393→    package com.test
 394→
 395→    import com.test.util
 396→
 397→    val a = "a"
 398→
 399→    fun b() {
 400→    }
 401→
 402→    fun c()
 403→    ```
 404→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 405→
 406→    ```kotlin
 407→    package com.test
 408→
 409→
 410→    import com.test.util
 411→
 412→
 413→    val a = "a"
 414→
 415→
 416→    fun b() {
 417→    }
 418→
 419→
 420→    fun c()

 422→ 
 423→Rule id: `no-consecutive-blank-lines` (`standard` rule set)
 424→
 425→## No empty (`{}`) class bodies
 426→
 427→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 428→
 429→    ```kotlin
 430→    class C
 431→    data class DC(val v: Any)
 432→    interface I
 433→    object O
 434→    ```
 435→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 436→
 437→    ```kotlin
 438→    class C {}
 439→    data class DC(val v: Any) { }
 440→    interface I {
 441→    }
 442→    object O{}
 443→    ```
 444→
 445→Rule id: `no-empty-class-body` (`standard` rule set)
 446→
 447→## No leading empty lines in method blocks
 448→
 449→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 450→
 451→    ```kotlin
 452→    fun bar() {
 453→       val a = 2
 454→    }
 455→    ```
 456→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 457→
 458→    ```kotlin
 459→    fun bar() {
 460→
 461→       val a = 2
 462→    }
 463→    ```
 464→
 465→Rule id: `no-empty-first-line-in-method-block` (`standard` rule set)
 466→
 467→## No line break after else
 468→
 469→Disallows line breaks after the else keyword if that could lead to confusion, for example:
 470→
 471→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 472→
 473→    ```kotlin
 474→    fun funA() {
 475→        if (conditionA()) {
 476→            doSomething()
 477→        } else if (conditionB()) {
 478→            doAnotherThing()
 479→        }
 480→    }
 481→    ```
 482→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 483→
 484→    ```kotlin
 485→    fun funA() {
 486→        if (conditionA()) {
 487→            doSomething()
 488→        } else
 489→        if (conditionB()) {
 490→            doAnotherThing()
 491→        }
 492→    }
 493→    ```
 494→
 495→Rule id: `no-line-break-after-else` (`standard` rule set)
 496→
 497→## No line break before assignment 
 498→
 499→When a line is broken at an assignment (`=`) operator the break comes after the symbol.
 500→
 501→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 502→
 503→    ```kotlin
 504→    val valA =
 505→        ""
 506→    ```
 507→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 508→
 509→    ```kotlin
 510→    val valA
 511→        = ""
 512→    ```
 513→
 514→Rule id: `no-line-break-before-assignment` (`standard` rule set)
 515→
 516→## No multi spaces
 517→
 518→Except in indentation and in KDoc's it is not allowed to have multiple consecutive spaces.
 519→
 520→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 521→
 522→    ```kotlin
 523→    fun main() {
 524→        x(1, 3)
 525→    }
 526→    ```
 527→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 528→
 529→    ```kotlin
 530→    fun  main()  {
 531→        x(1,  3)
 532→    }
 533→    ```
 534→
 535→Rule id: `no-multi-spaces` (`standard` rule set)
 536→
 537→## No semicolons
 538→
 539→No semicolons (unless used to separate multiple statements on the same line).
 540→
 541→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 542→
 543→    ```kotlin
 544→    fun foo() {
 545→        bar()
 546→
 547→        bar()
 548→    }
 549→    ```
 550→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 551→
 552→    ```kotlin
 553→    fun foo() {
 554→        ;
 555→        bar()
 556→        ;
 557→
 558→        bar()
 559→
 560→        ;
 561→    }
 562→    ```
 563→
 564→Rule id: `no-semi` (`standard` rule set)
 565→
 566→## No trailing whitespaces
 567→
 568→Rule id: `no-trailing-spaces` (`standard` rule set)
 569→
 570→## No `Unit` as return type 
 571→
 572→The `Unit` type is not allowed as return type of a function.
 573→returns (`fun fn {}` instead of `fun fn: Unit {}`)
 574→
 575→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 576→
 577→    ```kotlin
 578→    fun fn() {}
 579→    ```
 580→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 581→
 582→    ```kotlin
 583→    fun fn(): Unit {}
 584→    ```
 585→
 586→Rule id: `no-unit-return` (`standard` rule set)
 587→
 588→## No unused imports
 589→
 590→!!! warning
 591→    This rule is not able to detect *all* unused imports as mentioned in this [issue comment](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1754#issuecomment-1368201667).
 592→
 593→Rule id: `no-unused-imports` (`standard` rule set)
 594→
 595→## No wildcard imports
 596→
 597→No wildcard imports except imports listed in `.editorconfig` property `ij_kotlin_packages_to_use_import_on_demand`.
 598→
 599→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 600→
 601→    ```kotlin
 602→    import foobar.Bar
 603→    import foobar.Foo
 604→    ```
 605→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 606→
 607→    ```kotlin
 608→    import foobar.*
 609→    ```
 610→
 611→!!! warning
 612→    In case property `ij_kotlin_packages_to_use_import_on_demand` is not explicitly set, it allows wildcards imports like `java.util.*` by default to keep in sync with IntelliJ IDEA behavior. To disallow *all* wildcard imports, add property below to your `.editorconfig`:
 613→    ```editorconfig
 614→    [*.{kt,kts}]
 615→    ij_kotlin_packages_to_use_import_on_demand = unset
 616→    ```
 617→
 618→Rule id: `no-wildcard-imports` (`standard` rule set)
 619→
 620→## Package name
 621→
 622→Validates that the package name matches the regular expression `[a-z][a-zA-Z\d]*(\.[a-z][a-zA-Z\d]*)*`.
 623→
 624→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 625→
 626→    ```kotlin
 627→    package foo
 628→    package foo.bar
 629→    ```
 630→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 631→
 632→    ```kotlin
 633→    package Foo
 634→    package foo.Foo
 635→    package `foo bar`
 636→    package foo.`foo bar`
 637→    ```
 638→
 639→Rule id: `package-name` (`standard` rule set)
 640→
 641→## Parameter list wrapping
 642→
 643→When class/function signature doesn't fit on a single line, each parameter must be on a separate line
 644→
 645→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 646→
 647→    ```kotlin
 648→    class ClassA(paramA: String, paramB: String, paramC: String)
 649→    class ClassA(
 650→        paramA: String,
 651→        paramB: String,
 652→        paramC: String
 653→    )
 654→    fun f(a: Any, b: Any, c: Any)
 655→    fun f(
 656→        a: Any,
 657→        b: Any,
 658→        c: Any
 659→    )
 660→    fun foo(
 661→        @Bar fooBar: FooBar
 662→    )
 663→    ```
 664→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed (ktlint_official)"
 665→
 666→    ```kotlin
 667→    class ClassA(
 668→        paramA: String, paramB: String,
 669→        paramC: String
 670→    )
 671→    fun f(
 672→        a: Any,
 673→        b: Any, c: Any
 674→    )
 675→    fun foo(@Bar fooBar: FooBar)
 676→    ```
 677→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed (non ktlint_official)""
 678→
 679→    ```kotlin
 680→    class ClassA(
 681→        paramA: String, paramB: String,
 682→        paramC: String
 683→    )
 684→    fun f(
 685→        a: Any,
 686→        b: Any, c: Any
 687→    )
 688→    ```
 689→
 690→Rule id: `parameter-list-wrapping` (`standard` rule set)
 691→
 692→## Parameter wrapping
 693→
 694→When a function or class parameter doesn't fit on a single line, wrap the type or value to a separate line
 695→
 696→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint (ktlint_official)"
 697→
 698→    ```kotlin
 699→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
 700→    // at the X character on the right           X
 701→    class Bar(
 702→        val fooooooooooooooooooooooooTooLong:
 703→            Foo,
 704→    )
 705→    fun bar(
 706→        fooooooooooooooooooooooooTooLong:
 707→            Foo,
 708→    )
 709→    ```
 710→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint (non ktlint_official)"
 711→
 712→    ```kotlin
 713→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
 714→    // at the X character on the right           X
 715→    class Bar(
 716→        val fooooooooooooooooooooooooTooLong:
 717→        Foo,
 718→    )
 719→    fun bar(
 720→        fooooooooooooooooooooooooTooLong:
 721→        Foo,
 722→    )
 723→    ```
 724→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 725→
 726→    ```kotlin
 727→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
 728→    // at the X character on the right           X
 729→    class Bar(
 730→        val fooooooooooooooooooooooooTooLong: Foo,
 731→    )
 732→    fun bar(
 733→        fooooooooooooooooooooooooooooTooLong: Foo,
 734→    )
 735→    ```
 736→
 737→Rule id: `parameter-wrapping` (`standard` rule set)
 738→
 739→## Property wrapping
 740→
 741→When a property doesn't fit on a single line, wrap the type or value to a separate line
 742→
 743→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 744→
 745→    ```kotlin
 746→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
 747→    // at the X character on the right           X
 748→    val aVariableWithALooooooooooooongName:
 749→        String
 750→    ```
 751→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 752→
 753→    ```kotlin
 754→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
 755→    // at the X character on the right           X
 756→    val aVariableWithALooooooooooooongName: String

 758→
 759→Rule id: `property-wrapping` (`standard` rule set)
 760→
 761→## String template
 762→
 763→Consistent string templates (`$v` instead of `${v}`, `${p.v}` instead of `${p.v.toString()}`)
 764→
 765→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 766→
 767→    ```kotlin
 768→    val foo = "$foo hello"
 769→    ```
 770→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 771→
 772→    ```kotlin
 773→    val foo = "${foo} hello"
 774→    ```
 775→
 776→Rule id: `string-template` (`standard` rule set)
 777→
 778→## Trailing comma on call site
 779→
 780→Consistent removal (default) or adding of trailing commas on call site.
 781→
 782→!!! important
 783→    KtLint uses the IntelliJ IDEA `.editorconfig` property `ij_kotlin_allow_trailing_comma_on_call_site` to configure the rule. When this property is enabled, KtLint *enforces* the usage of the trailing comma at call site while IntelliJ IDEA default formatter only *allows* to use the trailing comma but leaves it to the developer's discretion to actually use it (or not). KtLint values *consistent* formatting more than a per-situation decision.
 784→
 785→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 786→
 787→    ```kotlin
 788→    FooWrapper(
 789→        Foo(
 790→            a = 3,
 791→            b = 4,
 792→        ),
 793→    )
 794→    ```
 795→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 796→
 797→    ```kotlin
 798→    FooWrapper(Foo(
 799→        a = 3,
 800→        b = 4,
 801→    ),) // it's weird to insert "," between unwrapped (continued) parenthesis
 802→    ```
 803→
 804→!!! note
 805→    In KtLint 0.48.x the default value for using the trailing comma on call site has been changed to `true` except when codestyle `android` is used.
 806→    
 807→    Although the [Kotlin coding conventions](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/reference/coding-conventions.html#trailing-commas) leaves it to the developer's discretion to use trailing commas on the call site, it also states that usage of trailing commas has several benefits:
 808→    
 809→     * It makes version-control diffs cleaner – as all the focus is on the changed value.
 810→     * It makes it easy to add and reorder elements – there is no need to add or delete the comma if you manipulate elements.
 811→     * It simplifies code generation, for example, for object initializers. The last element can also have a comma.
 812→
 813→!!! note
 814→    Trailing comma on call site is automatically disabled if the [Wrapping](#wrapping) rule (or, before version `0.45.0`, the [Indentation](#indentation) rule) is disabled or not loaded. Because it cannot provide proper formatting with unwrapped calls. (see [dependencies](./dependencies.md)).
 815→
 816→Rule id: `trailing-comma-on-call-site` (`standard` rule set)
 817→
 818→## Trailing comma on declaration site
 819→
 820→Consistent removal (default) or adding of trailing commas on declaration site.
 821→
 822→!!! important
 823→    KtLint uses the IntelliJ IDEA `.editorconfig` property `ij_kotlin_allow_trailing_comma` to configure the rule. When this property is enabled, KtLint *enforces* the usage of the trailing comma at declaration site while IntelliJ IDEA default formatter only *allows* to use the trailing comma but leaves it to the developer's discretion to actually use it (or not). KtLint values *consistent* formatting more than a per-situation decision.
 824→
 825→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 826→
 827→    ```kotlin
 828→    class FooWrapper(
 829→        val foo = Foo(
 830→            a = 3,
 831→            b = 4,
 832→        ),
 833→    )
 834→    ```
 835→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 836→
 837→    ```kotlin
 838→    class FooWrapper(val foo = Foo(
 839→        a = 3,
 840→        b = 4,
 841→    ),) // it's weird to insert "," between unwrapped (continued) parenthesis
 842→    ```
 843→
 844→!!! note
 845→    In KtLint 0.48.x the default value for using the trailing comma on declaration site has been changed to `true` except when codestyle `android` is used.
 846→
 847→    The [Kotlin coding conventions](https://kotlinlang.org/docs/reference/coding-conventions.html#trailing-commas) encourages the usage of trailing commas on the declaration site, but leaves it to the developer's discretion to use trailing commas on the call site. But next to this, it also states that usage of trailing commas has several benefits:
 848→    
 849→     * It makes version-control diffs cleaner – as all the focus is on the changed value.
 850→     * It makes it easy to add and reorder elements – there is no need to add or delete the comma if you manipulate elements.
 851→     * It simplifies code generation, for example, for object initializers. The last element can also have a comma.
 852→
 853→!!! note
 854→    Trailing comma on declaration site is automatically disabled if the [Wrapping](#wrapping) rule (or, before version `0.45.0`, the [Indentation](#indentation) rule) is disabled or not loaded. Because it cannot provide proper formatting with unwrapped declarations. (see [dependencies](./dependencies.md)).
 855→
 856→Rule id: `trailing-comma-on-declaration-site` (`standard` rule set)
 857→
 858→## Unnecessary parenthesis before trailing lambda
 859→
 860→An empty parentheses block before a lambda is redundant.
 861→
 862→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 863→
 864→    ```kotlin
 865→    "some-string".count { it == '-' }
 866→    ```
 867→
 868→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 869→
 870→    ```kotlin
 871→    "some-string".count() { it == '-' }
 872→    ```
 873→
 874→Rule id: `unnecessary-parentheses-before-trailing-lambda` (`standard` rule set)

 876→## Wrapping
 877→
 878→### Wrapping
 879→
 880→Inserts missing newlines (for example between parentheses of a multi-line function call).
 881→
 882→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 883→
 884→    ```kotlin
 885→    val x = f(
 886→        a,
 887→        b,
 888→        c
 889→    )
 890→    ```
 891→
 892→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 893→
 894→    ```kotlin
 895→    val x = f(
 896→        a,
 897→        b,
 898→        c)
 899→    ```
 900→
 901→Rule id: `wrapping` (`standard` rule set)
 902→
 903→### Comment wrapping
 904→
 905→A block comment should start and end on a line that does not contain any other element.
 906→
 907→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 908→
 909→    ```kotlin
 910→    /* Some comment 1 */
 911→    val foo1 = "foo1"
 912→    val foo2 = "foo" // Some comment
 913→    val foo3 = { /* no-op */ } 
 914→    ```
 915→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 916→
 917→    ```kotlin
 918→    /* Some comment 1 */ val foo1 = "foo1"
 919→    val foo2 = "foo" /* Block comment instead of end-of-line comment */
 920→    val foo3 = "foo" /* Some comment
 921→                      * with a newline
 922→                      */
 923→    ```
 924→
 925→Rule id: `comment-wrapping` (`standard` rule set)
 926→
 927→## Spacing
 928→
 929→### Angle bracket spacing
 930→

 935→    ```kotlin

 937→    val b: Map<Int, String> = mapOf()
 938→    val c: Map<Int, String> = mapOf()
 939→    ```
 940→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 941→
 942→    ```kotlin

 944→    val b: Map<Int, String > = mapOf()
 945→    val c: Map <Int, String> = mapOf()
 946→    ```

 948→Rule id: `spacing-around-angle-brackets` (`standard` rule set)
 949→
 950→### Annotation spacing
 951→
 952→Annotations should be separated by a single line break.

 961→     * block comment
 962→     */

 964→    class FooBar {
 965→    }
 966→    ```
 967→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 968→
 969→    ```kotlin
 970→    @JvmField
 971→
 972→    fun foo() {}
 973→
 974→    @Foo @Bar
 975→    /**
 976→     * block comment
 977→     */
 978→    class FooBar {
 979→    }
 980→    ```
 981→

1→!!! important
  2→    Up and until Ktlint version `0.47`, experimental were located in a separate `experimental` rule set. As of Ktlint version `0.48`, each rule set can optionally define experimental rules.
  3→
  4→All experimental rules described below are part of the `standard` rule set of Ktlint. To enable all experimental rules (from all rule sets), set `editorconfig` property below:
  5→```ini
  6→[*.{kt,kts}]
  7→ktlint_experimental=enabled
  8→```
  9→Also see [enable/disable specific rules](../configuration-ktlint/#disabled-rules).

 11→## Binary expression wrapping
 12→
 13→Wraps binary expression at the operator reference whenever the binary expression does not fit on the line. In case the binary expression is nested, the expression is evaluated from outside to inside. If the left and right hand sides of the binary expression, after wrapping, fit on a single line then the inner binary expressions will not be wrapped. If one or both inner binary expression still do not fit on a single after wrapping of the outer binary expression, then each of those inner binary expressions will be wrapped.
 14→
 15→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 16→
 17→    ```kotlin
 18→    fun foo() {
 19→        // Assume that the last allowed character is
 20→        // at the X character on the right                       X
 21→        if ((leftHandSideExpression && rightHandSideExpression) ||
 22→            (leftHandSideLongExpression &&
 23→                rightHandSideLongExpression)) {
 24→            // do something
 25→        }
 26→    }
 27→    ```
 28→
 29→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 30→
 31→    ```kotlin
 32→    fun foo() {
 33→        // Assume that the last allowed character is
 34→        // at the X character on the right                       X
 35→        if ((leftHandSideExpression && rightHandSideExpression) || (leftHandSideLongExpression && rightHandSideLongExpression)) {
 36→            // do something
 37→        }
 38→    }
 39→    ```
 40→
 41→Rule id: `binary-expression-wrapping` (`standard` rule set)
 42→
 43→## Blank line before declarations
 44→
 45→Requires a blank line before any class or function declaration. No blank line is required between the class signature and the first declaration in the class. In a similar way, a blank line is required before any list of top level or class properties. No blank line is required before local properties or between consecutive properties.
 46→
 47→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
 48→
 49→    ```kotlin
 50→    const val foo1 = "foo1"
 51→
 52→    class FooBar {
 53→        val foo2 = "foo2"
 54→        val foo3 = "foo3"
 55→
 56→        fun bar1() {
 57→           val foo4 = "foo4"
 58→           val foo5 = "foo5"
 59→        }
 60→
 61→        fun bar2() = "bar"
 62→
 63→        val foo6 = "foo3"
 64→        val foo7 = "foo4"
 65→
 66→        enum class Foo {}
 67→    }
 68→    ```
 69→
 70→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
 71→
 72→    ```kotlin
 73→    const val foo1 = "foo1"
 74→    class FooBar {
 75→        val foo2 = "foo2"
 76→        val foo3 = "foo3"
 77→        fun bar1() {
 78→           val foo4 = "foo4"
 79→           val foo5 = "foo5"
 80→        }
 81→        fun bar2() = "bar"
 82→        val foo6 = "foo3"
 83→        val foo7 = "foo4"
 84→        enum class Foo {}
 85→    }
 86→    ```
 87→
 88→Rule id: `blank-line-before-declaration` (`standard` rule set)
 89→
 90→!!! Note
 91→    This rule is only run when `ktlint_code_style` is set to `ktlint_official` or when the rule is enabled explicitly.

 93→## Discouraged comment location
 94→
 95→Detect discouraged comment locations (no autocorrect).
 96→
 97→!!! note
 98→    Kotlin allows comments to be placed almost everywhere. As this can lead to code which is hard to read, most of them will never be used in practice. Ideally each rule takes comments at all possible locations into account. Sometimes this is really hard and not worth the effort. By explicitly forbidding such comment locations, the development of those rules becomes a bit easier. 
 99→
100→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
101→
102→    ```kotlin
103→    fun <T> /* some comment */ foo(t: T) = "some-result"
104→
105→    fun foo() {
106→        if (true)
107→            // some comment
108→            bar()
109→    }
110→    ```
111→
112→Rule id: `discouraged-comment-location` (`standard` rule set)
113→
114→## Disallow empty lines at start of class body
115→
116→Detect blank lines at start of a class body.
117→
118→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
119→
120→    ```kotlin
121→    class Foo {
122→        val foo = "foo"
123→    }
124→    ```
125→
126→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
127→
128→    ```kotlin
129→    class Foo {
130→
131→        val foo = "foo"
132→    }
133→    ```
134→
135→Rule id: `no-empty-first-line-in-class-body` (`standard` rule set)
136→
137→!!! Note
138→    This rule is only run when `ktlint_code_style` is set to `ktlint_official` or when the rule is enabled explicitly.

140→## Disallow consecutive comments
141→
142→Consecutive comments are disallowed in following cases:
143→- Any mix of a consecutive kdoc, a block comment or an EOL comment unless separated by a blank line in between
144→- Consecutive KDocs (even when separated by a blank line)
145→- Consecutive block comments (even when separated by a blank line)
146→
147→Consecutive EOL comments are always allowed as they are often used instead of a block comment.
148→
149→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
150→
151→    ```kotlin
152→    // An EOL comment
153→    // may be followed by another EOL comment
154→    val foo = "foo"
155→
156→    // Different comment types (including KDoc) may be consecutive ..
157→
158→    /*
159→     * ... but do need to be separated by a blank line ...
160→     */
161→
162→    /**
163→      * ... but a KDoc can not be followed by an EOL or a block comment or another KDoc
164→      */
165→    fun bar() = "bar"
166→
167→    ```
168→
169→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
170→
171→    ```kotlin
172→    /*
173→     * Block comments can not be consecutive ...
174→     */
175→    /*
176→     * ... even not when separated by a new line.
177→     */
178→    val bar = "bar" 
179→
180→    /**
181→      * A KDoc can not be followed by a block comment or an EOL comment or another KDOC
182→      */
183→
184→    // ... even not when separated by a new line.
185→    ```
186→
187→Rule id: `no-consecutive-comments` (`standard` rule set)
188→
189→!!! Note
190→    This rule is only run when `ktlint_code_style` is set to `ktlint_official` or when the rule is enabled explicitly.
191→
192→## Function signature
193→
194→Rewrites the function signature to a single line when possible (e.g. when not exceeding the `max_line_length` property) or a multiline signature otherwise. In case of function with a body expression, the body expression is placed on the same line as the function signature when not exceeding the `max_line_length` property. Optionally the function signature can be forced to be written as a multiline signature in case the function has more than a specified number of parameters (`.editorconfig` property `ktlint_function_signature_wrapping_rule_always_with_minimum_parameters`)
195→
196→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
197→
198→    ```kotlin
199→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
200→    // at the X character on the right           X
201→    fun foooooooo(
202→        a: Any,
203→        b: Any,
204→        c: Any
205→    ): String {
206→        // body
207→    }
208→
209→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
210→    // at the X character on the right           X
211→    fun bar(a: Any, b: Any, c: Any): String {
212→        // body
213→    }
214→
215→    // When wrapping of body is set to 'default'.
216→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
217→    // at the X character on the right           X
218→    fun f(a: Any, b: Any): String = "some-result"
219→        .uppercase()
220→
221→    // When wrapping of body is set to 'multiline'
222→    // or 'always'.
223→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
224→    // at the X character on the right           X
225→    fun f(a: Any, b: Any): String =
226→        "some-result"
227→            .uppercase()
228→    ```
229→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
230→
231→    ```kotlin
232→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
233→    // at the X character on the right           X
234→    fun foooooooo(a: Any, b: Any, c: Any): String {
235→        // body
236→    }
237→
238→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
239→    // at the X character on the right           X
240→    fun bar(
241→        a: Any,
242→        b: Any,
243→        c: Any
244→    ): String {
245→        // body
246→    }
247→
248→    // When wrapping of body is set to 'default'.
249→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
250→    // at the X character on the right           X
251→    fun f(a: Any, b: Any): String =
252→        "some-result"
253→            .uppercase()
254→
255→    // When wrapping of body is set to 'multiline'
256→    // or 'always'.
257→    // Assume that the last allowed character is
258→    // at the X character on the right           X
259→    fun f(a: Any, b: Any): String = "some-result"
260→        .uppercase()
261→    ```
262→
263→Rule id: `function-signature` (`standard` rule set)
264→
265→## If else bracing
266→
267→If at least one branch of an if-else statement or an if-else-if statement is wrapped between curly braces then all branches should be wrapped between braces.
268→
269→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
270→
271→    ```kotlin
272→    fun foo(value: int) {
273→        if (value > 0) {
274→            doSomething()
275→        } else if (value < 0) {
276→            doSomethingElse()
277→        } else {
278→            doSomethingElse2()
279→        }
280→    }
281→    ```
282→
283→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
284→
285→    ```kotlin
286→    fun foo(value: int) {
287→        if (value > 0)
288→            doSomething()
289→        else if (value < 0) {
290→            doSomethingElse()
291→        } else
292→            doSomethingElse2()
293→    }
294→    ```
295→
296→Rule id: `if-else-bracing` (`standard` rule set)
297→
298→!!! Note
299→    This rule is only run when `ktlint_code_style` is set to `ktlint_official` or when the rule is enabled explicitly.
300→
301→## If else wrapping
302→
303→A single line if-statement should be kept simple. It may contain no more than one else-branch. The branches may not be wrapped in a block.
304→
305→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
306→
307→    ```kotlin
308→    fun foobar() {
309→        if (true) foo()
310→        if (true) foo() else bar()
311→    }
312→    ```
313→
314→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
315→
316→    ```kotlin
317→    fun foobar() {
318→        if (true) if (false) foo() else bar()
319→        if (true) bar() else if (false) foo() else bar()
320→        if (true) { foo() } else bar()
321→        if (true) bar() else { if (false) foo() else bar() }
322→    }
323→    ```
324→
325→Rule id: `if-else-wrapping` (`standard` rule set)
326→
327→!!! Note
328→    This rule is only run when `ktlint_code_style` is set to `ktlint_official` or when the rule is enabled explicitly.
329→
330→## Naming
331→
332→### Function naming
333→
334→Enforce naming of function. 
335→
336→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
337→
338→    ```kotlin
339→    fun foo() {}
340→    fun fooBar() {}
341→    ```
342→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint Test"
343→
344→    ```kotlin
345→    @Test
346→    fun `Some name`() {}
347→
348→    @Test
349→    fun do_something() {}
350→    ```
351→
352→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
353→
354→    ```kotlin
355→    fun Foo() {}
356→    fun Foo_Bar() {}
357→    fun `Some name`() {}
358→    fun do_something() {}
359→    ```
360→
361→!!! note
362→    Functions in files which import a class from package `org.junit`, `org.testng` or `kotlin.test` are considered to be test functions. Functions in such classes are allowed to have underscores in the name. Or function names can be specified between backticks and do not need to adhere to the normal naming convention.
363→
364→This rule can also be suppressed with the IntelliJ IDEA inspection suppression `FunctionName`.
365→
366→Rule id: `function-naming` (`standard` rule set)
367→
368→### Package naming
369→
370→Enforce naming of package.
371→
372→This rule can also be suppressed with the IntelliJ IDEA inspection suppression `PackageName`.
373→
374→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
375→
376→    ```kotlin
377→    package foo
378→    package foo.foo
379→    package foo_bar
380→    package foo.foo_bar
381→    ```
382→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
383→
384→    ```kotlin
385→    package Foo
386→    package foo.Foo
387→    package `foo bar`
388→    package foo.`foo bar`
389→    ```
390→
391→Rule id: `package-naming` (`standard` rule set)
392→
393→### Property naming
394→
395→Enforce naming of property.
396→
397→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
398→
399→    ```kotlin
400→    val FOO_1 = Foo()
401→    val FOO_BAR_1 = "FOO-BAR"
402→
403→    var foo1: Foo = Foo()
404→
405→    class Bar {
406→        const val FOO_2 = "foo"
407→        const val FOO_BAR_2 = "FOO-BAR"
408→
409→        val foo2 = "foo"
410→        val fooBar2 = "foo-bar"
411→
412→        // Backing property
413→        private val _elementList = mutableListOf<Element>()
414→        val elementList: List<Element>
415→            get() = _elementList
416→    }
417→    ```
418→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
419→
420→    ```kotlin
421→    val Foo1 = Foo()
422→    val FooBar1 = "FOO-BAR"
423→
424→    var FOO_1: Foo = Foo()
425→
426→    class Bar {
427→        const val foo2 = "foo"
428→        const val fooBar2 = "FOO-BAR"
429→
430→        val FOO2 = "foo"
431→        val FOO_BAR_2 = "foo-bar"
432→
433→        // Incomplete backing property as public property 'elementList1' is missing
434→        private val _elementList1 = mutableListOf<Element>()
435→
436→        // Invalid backing property as '_elementList2' is not a private property
437→        val _elementList2 = mutableListOf<Element>()
438→        val elementList2: List<Element>
439→            get() = _elementList2
440→    }
441→    ```
442→
443→!!! note
444→    Top level `val` properties and `const val` properties have to be written in screaming snake notation. Local `val` and `const val` are written in lower camel case.
445→
446→This rule can also be suppressed with the IntelliJ IDEA inspection suppression `PropertyName`.
447→
448→Rule id: `property-naming` (`standard` rule set)
449→
450→## No empty file
451→
452→A kotlin (script) file should not be empty. It needs to contain at least one declaration. Files only contain a package and/or import statements are as of that disallowed.
453→
454→Rule id: `no-empty-file`

662→### String template indent
663→
664→Enforce consistent string template indentation for multiline string templates which are post-fixed with `.trimIndent()`. The opening and closing `"""` are placed on separate lines and the indentation of the content of the template is aligned with the `"""`.
665→
666→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
667→
668→    ```kotlin
669→    val foo =
670→        """
671→        line1
672→        line2
673→        """.trimIndent()
674→    fun foo() {
675→        // The opening """ can not be wrapped to next line as that would result in a compilation error
676→        return """
677→            line1
678→            line2
679→            """.trimIndent()
680→    }
681→    ```
682→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
683→
684→    ```kotlin
685→    val foo = """
686→              line1
687→              line2
688→              """.trimIndent()
689→    fun foo() {
690→        return """
691→            line1
692→            line2
693→        """.trimIndent()
694→    }
695→    ```
696→
697→Rule id: `string-template-indent` (`standard` rule set)
698→
699→!!! Note
700→    This rule is only run when `ktlint_code_style` is set to `ktlint_official` or when the rule is enabled explicitly.
701→
702→### Try catch finally spacing
703→
704→Enforce consistent spacing in `try { .. } catch { .. } finally { .. }`.
705→
706→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
707→
708→    ```kotlin
709→    fun foo() =
710→        try {
711→            // do something
712→        } catch (exception: Exception) {
713→            // handle exception
714→        } finally {
715→            // clean up
716→        }
717→    ```
718→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
719→
720→    ```kotlin
721→    fun foo1() = try { /* ... */ } catch (exception: Exception) { /* ... */ } finally { /* ... */ }
722→    fun foo2() = 
723→        try {
724→            // do something
725→        }
726→        catch (exception: Exception) {
727→            // handle exception
728→        }
729→        finally {
730→            // clean up
731→        }
732→    ```
733→
734→Rule id: `try-catch-finally-spacing` (`standard` rule set)
735→
736→!!! Note
737→    This rule is only run when `ktlint_code_style` is set to `ktlint_official` or when the rule is enabled explicitly.
738→
739→### Type argument list spacing
740→
741→Spacing before and after the angle brackets of a type argument list.
742→
743→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
744→
745→    ```kotlin
746→    val res = ArrayList<LintError>()
747→    class B<T> : A<T>() {
748→        override fun x() = super<A>.x()
749→    }
750→    ```
751→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
752→
753→    ```kotlin
754→    val res = ArrayList < LintError > ()
755→    class B<T> : A< T >() {
756→        override fun x() = super< A >.x()
757→    }
758→    ```
759→
760→Rule id: `type-argument-list-spacing` (`standard` rule set)
761→
762→### Type parameter list spacing
763→
764→Spacing after a type parameter list in function and class declarations.
765→
766→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
767→
768→    ```kotlin
769→    fun <T> foo1(t: T) = "some-result"
770→    fun <T> foo2(t: T) = "some-result"
771→    fun <T> foo3(t: T) = "some-result"
772→    ```
773→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
774→
775→    ```kotlin
776→    fun<T> foo1(t: T) = "some-result"
777→    fun <T>foo2(t: T) = "some-result"
778→    fun<T>foo3(t: T) = "some-result"
779→    ```
780→
781→Rule id: `type-parameter-list-spacing` (`standard` rule set)
782→
783→## Wrapping
784→
785→### Content receiver wrapping
786→
787→Wraps the content receiver list to a separate line regardless of maximum line length. If the maximum line length is configured and is exceeded, wrap the context receivers and if needed its projection types to separate lines.
788→
789→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
790→
791→    ```kotlin
792→    // ALways wrap regardless of whether max line length is set
793→    context(Foo)
794→    fun fooBar()
795→
796→    // Wrap each context receiver to a separate line when the
797→    // entire context receiver list does not fit on a single line
798→    context(
799→        Fooooooooooooooooooo1,
800→        Foooooooooooooooooooooooooooooo2
801→    )
802→    fun fooBar()
803→
804→    // Wrap each context receiver to a separate line when the
805→    // entire context receiver list does not fit on a single line.
806→    // Also, wrap each of it projection types in case a context
807→    // receiver does not fit on a single line after it has been
808→    // wrapped.
809→    context(
810→        Foooooooooooooooo<
811→            Foo,
812→            Bar
813→            >
814→    )
815→    fun fooBar()
816→    ```
817→
818→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
819→
820→    ```kotlin
821→    // Should be wrapped regardless of whether max line length is set
822→    context(Foo) fun fooBar()
823→
824→    // Should be wrapped when the entire context receiver list does not
825→    // fit on a single line
826→    context(Fooooooooooooooooooo1, Foooooooooooooooooooooooooooooo2)
827→    fun fooBar()
828→
829→    // Should be wrapped when the entire context receiver list does not
830→    // fit on a single line. Also, it should wrap each of it projection
831→    // type in case a context receiver does not fit on a single line 
832→    // after it has been wrapped.
833→    context(Foooooooooooooooo<Foo, Bar>)
834→    fun fooBar()
835→    ```
836→
837→Rule id: `context-receiver-wrapping` (`standard` rule set)
838→
839→### Enum wrapping
840→
841→An enum should be a single line, or each enum entry has to be placed on a separate line. In case the enumeration contains enum entries and declarations those are to be separated by a blank line.
842→
843→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
844→
845→    ```kotlin
846→    enum class Foo { A, B, C, D }
847→
848→    enum class Foo {
849→        A,
850→        B,
851→        C,
852→        D,
853→        ;
854→
855→        fun foo() = "foo"
856→    }
857→    ```
858→
859→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
860→
861→    ```kotlin
862→    enum class Foo {
863→        A,
864→        B, C,
865→        D
866→    }
867→
868→    enum class Foo {
869→        A;
870→        fun foo() = "foo"
871→    }
872→    ```
873→
874→Rule id: `enum-wrapping` (`standard` rule set)
875→
876→### Multiline expression wrapping
877→
878→Multiline expression on the right hand side of an expression are forced to start on a separate line. Expressions in return statement are excluded as that would result in a compilation error. 
879→
880→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
881→
882→    ```kotlin
883→    val foo =
884→        foo(
885→            parameterName =
886→                "The quick brown fox "
887→                    .plus("jumps ")
888→                    .plus("over the lazy dog"),
889→        )
890→    ```
891→
892→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
893→
894→    ```kotlin
895→    val foo = foo(
896→        parameterName = "The quick brown fox "
897→            .plus("jumps ")
898→            .plus("over the lazy dog"),
899→    )
900→    ```
901→
902→Rule id: `multiline-expression-wrapping` (`standard` rule set)
903→
904→!!! Note
905→    This rule is only run when `ktlint_code_style` is set to `ktlint_official` or when the rule is enabled explicitly.
906→
907→### Statement wrapping
908→
909→A function, class/object body or other block body statement has to be placed on different line than the braces of the body block.
910→
911→=== "[:material-heart:](#) Ktlint"
912→
913→    ```kotlin
914→    fun foo() {
915→        if (true) {
916→            // do something
917→        }
918→    }
919→    class A {
920→        val a = 0
921→        val b = 1
922→    }
923→    ```
924→
925→=== "[:material-heart-off-outline:](#) Disallowed"
926→
927→    ```kotlin
928→    fun foo() { if (true) {
929→            // do something
930→        }
931→    }
932→    class A { val a = 0
933→        val b = 1 }
934→    ```
935→
936→Rule id: `statement-wrapping`

1→# Changelog
  2→All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.
  3→This project adheres to [Semantic Versioning](https://semver.org/).
  4→
  5→## Unreleased
  6→
  7→### Custom Rule Providers need to prepare for Kotlin 1.9
  8→
  9→In Kotlin 1.9 the extension points of the embedded kotlin compiler will change. Ktlint only uses the `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` extension point. This extension is not yet supported in 1.9, neither is it documented ([#KT-58704](https://youtrack.jetbrains.com/issue/KT-58704/Support-and-document-extension-point-org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler)). Without this extension point it might happen that your custom rules will throw exceptions during runtime. See [#1981](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1981).
 10→
 11→In Ktlint, 7 out of 77 rules needed small and sometimes bigger changes to become independent of the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler`. The impact on your custom rules may vary dependent on the way the autocorrect has been implemented. When manipulating `ASTNode`s there seems to be no impact. When, manipulating `PsiElement`s, some functions consistently result in a runtime exception.
 12→
 13→Based on the refactoring of the rules as provided by `ktlint-ruleset-standard` in Ktlint `0.49.x` the suggested refactoring is as follows:
 14→
 15→* Replace `LeafElement.replaceWithText(String)` with `LeafElement.rawReplaceWithText(String)`.
 16→* Replace `PsiElement.addAfter(PsiElement, PsiElement)` with `AstNode.addChild(AstNode, AstNode)`. Note that this method inserts the new node (first) argument *before* the second argument node and as of that is not a simple replacement of the `PsiElement.addAfter(PsiElement, PsiElement)`.
 17→* Replace `PsiElement.replace(PsiElement)` with a sequence of `AstNode.addChild(AstNode, AstNode)` and `AstNode.removeChild(AstNode)`.
 18→
 19→Be aware that your custom rules might use other functions which also throw exceptions when the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` is no longer supported.
 20→
 21→In order to help you to analyse and fix the problems with your custom rules, ktlint temporarily supports to disable the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` using a flag. This flag is available in the Ktlint CLI and in the `KtlintRuleEngine`. By default, the extension point is enabled like it was in previous versions of ktlint.
 22→
 23→At least you should analyse the problems by running your test suits by running ktlint and disabling the extension point. Next you can start with fixing and releasing the updated rules. All rules in this version of ktlint have already been refactored and are not dependent on the extension point anymore. In Ktlint CLI the flag is to be activated with parameter `--disable-kotlin-extension-point`. API Consumers that use the `KtlintRuleEngine` directly, have to set property `enableKotlinCompilerExtensionPoint` to `false`.
 24→
 25→At this point in time, it is not yet decided what the next steps will be. Ktlint might drop the support of the extension points entirely. Or, if the extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` is fully supported at the time that ktlint will be based on kotlin 1.9 it might be kept. In either case, the flag will be dropped in a next version of ktlint.
 26→
 27→### Added
 28→
 29→* Add new experimental rule `binary-expression-wrapping`. This rule wraps a binary expression in case the max line length is exceeded ([#1940](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1940))
 30→* Add flag to disable extension point `org.jetbrains.kotlin.com.intellij.treeCopyHandler` to analyse impact on custom rules [#1981](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1981)
 31→* Add new experimental rule `no-empty-file` for all code styles. A kotlin (script) file may not be empty ([#1074](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1074))
 32→* Add new experimental rule `statement-wrapping` which ensures function, class, or other blocks statement body doesn't start or end at starting or ending braces of the block ([#1938](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1938))
 33→* Add new experimental rule `blank-line-before-declaration`. This rule requires a blank line before class, function or property declarations ([#1939](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1939))
 34→* Wrap multiple statements on same line `wrapping` ([#1078](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1078))
 35→* Inform user about using `--format` option of KtLint CLI when finding a violation that can be autocorrected ([#1071](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1071))
 36→
 37→### Removed
 38→
 39→### Fixed
 40→
 41→* Do not flag a (potential) mutable extension property in case the getter is annotated or prefixed with a modifier `property-naming` ([#2024](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2024))
 42→* Do not merge an annotated expression body with the function signature even if it fits on a single line ([#2043](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2043))
 43→* Ignore property with name `serialVersionUID` in `property-naming` ([#2045](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2045))
 44→* Prevent incorrect reporting of violations in case a nullable function type reference exceeds the maximum line length `parameter-list-wrapping` ([#1324](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1324)) 
 45→* Prevent false negative on `else` branch when body contains only chained calls or binary expression ([#2057](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2057))
 46→
 47→### Changed
 48→
 49→* Fix Java interoperability issues with `RuleId` and `RuleSetId` classes. Those classes were defined as value classes in `0.49.0` and `0.49.1`. Although the classes were marked with `@JvmInline` it seems that it is not possible to uses those classes from Java base API Consumers like Spotless. The classes have now been replaced with data classes ([#2041](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2041))
 50→* Update dependency `info.picocli:picocli` to v4.7.4
 51→* Update dependency `org.junit.jupiter:junit-jupiter` to v5.9.3
 52→* Update Kotlin development version to `1.8.22` and Kotlin version to `1.8.22`.
 53→* Update dependency io.github.detekt.sarif4k:sarif4k to v0.4.0
 54→* Update dependency org.jetbrains.dokka:dokka-gradle-plugin to v1.8.20
 55→* Run format up to 3 times in case formatting introduces changes which also can be autocorrected ([#2084](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2084))
 56→
 57→## [0.49.1] - 2023-05-12
 58→
 59→### Added
 60→
 61→### Removed
 62→
 63→### Fixed
 64→* Store path of file containing a lint violation relative to the location of the baseline file itself ([#1962](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1962))
 65→* Print absolute path of file in lint violations when flag "--relative" is not specified in Ktlint CLI ([#1963](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1963)) 
 66→* Handle parameter `--code-style=android_studio` in Ktlint CLI identical to deprecated parameter `--android` ([#1982](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1982))
 67→* Prevent nullpointer exception (NPE) if class without body is followed by multiple blank lines until end of file `no-consecutive-blank-lines` ([#1987](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1987))
 68→* Allow to 'unset' the `.editorconfig` property `ktlint_function_signature_rule_force_multiline_when_parameter_count_greater_or_equal_than` when using `ktlint_official` code style `function-signature` ([#1977](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1977))
 69→* Prevent nullpointer exception (NPE) if or operator at start of line is followed by dot qualified expression `indent` ([#1993](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1993))
 70→* Fix indentation of multiline parameter list in function literal `indent` ([#1976](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1976))
 71→* Restrict indentation of closing quotes to `ktlint_official` code style to keep formatting of other code styles consistent with `0.48.x` and before `indent` ([#1971](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1971))
 72→* Extract rule `no-single-line-block-comment` from `comment-wrapping` rule. The `no-single-line-block-comment` rule is added as experimental rule to the `ktlint_official` code style, but it can be enabled explicitly for the other code styles as well. ([#1980](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1980))
 73→* Clean-up unwanted logging dependencies ([#1998](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1998))
 74→* Fix directory traversal for patterns referring to paths outside of current working directory or any of it child directories ([#2002](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/2002))
 75→* Prevent multiple expressions on same line separated by semicolon ([#1078](https://github.com/pinterest/ktlint/issues/1078))
 76→
 77→### Changed
 78→
 79→* Moved class `Baseline` from `ktlint-cli` to `ktlint-cli-reporter-baseline` so that Baseline functionality is reusable for API Consumers.
 80→
 81→## [0.49.0] - 2023-04-21
 82→
 83→WARNING: This version of KtLint contains a number of breaking changes in KtLint CLI and KtLint API. If you are using KtLint with custom ruleset jars or custom reporter jars, then those need to be upgraded before you can use them with this version of ktlint. Please contact the maintainers of those jars and ask them to upgrade a.s.a.p.
 84→
 85→All rule id's in the output of Ktlint are now prefixed with a rule set. Although KtLint currently supports standard rules to be unqualified, users are encouraged to include the rule set id in all references to rules. This includes following:
 86→* The `--disabled-rules` parameter in KtLint CLI.
 87→* The `.editorconfig` properties used to enable or disable rule and rule sets. Note that properties `disabled_rules` and `ktlint_disabled_rules` have been removed in this release. See [disabled rules](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/configuration-ktlint/#disabled-rules) for more information.
 88→* The `source` element in the KtLint CLI `baseline.xml` file. Regenerating this file, fixes all rule references automatically.
 89→* The KtLint disable directives `ktlint-enable` / `ktlint-disable` and the `@Suppress('ktlint:...')` annotations.
 90→* The `VisitorModifier.RunAfterRule`.
 91→
 92→### Moving experimental rules to standard rule set
 93→
 94→The `experimental` rule set has been merged with `standard` rule set. The rules which formerly were part of the `experimental` rule set are still being treated as before. The rules will only be run in case `.editorconfig` property `ktlint_experimental` is enabled or in case the rule is explicitly enabled.
 95→
 96→Note that the prefix `experimental:` has to be removed from all references to this rule. Check references in:
 97→* The `--disabled-rules` parameter in KtLint CLI.
 98→* The `.editorconfig` properties used to enable or disable rule and rule sets. Note that properties `disabled_rules` and `ktlint_disabled_rules` have been removed in this release. See [disabled rules](https://pinterest.github.io/ktlint/rules/configuration-ktlint/#disabled-rules) for more information.
 99→* The KtLint disable directives `ktlint-enable` / `ktlint-disable` and the `@Suppress('ktlint:...')` annotations.
100→* The `VisitorModifier.RunAfterRule`.
